narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile shows a mix of strengths and challenges that the admissions model carefully evaluated. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 sits a bit below the typical 3.21 average, while your LSAT score of 29 is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.70, and your Year 1 school decile ranking of 5 is close to average at 5.71. The model predicts a 45.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application—law schools use this prediction to prioritize candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 3, well below the average of 5.53, signaling to the model a significant dip in academic performance later in school that often correlates with bar struggles. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward passing because, despite being below average, it showed enough foundational strength to mitigate some risk. Your Year 1 decile ranking also helped in this direction, as its near-average standing suggested early promise that the model viewed favorably. Overall, while your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of successful students (over 92%), the sharp drop in your later school performance outweighed these positives compared to typical admits who maintain steadier upward trajectories. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background—many in similar situations succeed with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average and strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to demonstrate upward momentum. Law schools value growth stories, so highlighting any explanations for your Year 3 dip in a revised application could help. You might also seek feedback from pre-law advisors on bolstering your GPA through graduate-level classes. With these steps, you can reshape your profile for stronger future predictions and admissions.
